content.legal_text: Chapter III: Citizenship
content.plain_english: This chapter of the Constitution defines who is considered a citizen of Nigeria and outlines the various ways citizenship can be acquired, lost, or renounced.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case often arises in discussions about the interpretation of citizenship provisions, particularly regarding dual citizenship and eligibility for public office.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Many countries, including the US, have constitutional or statutory provisions defining citizenship by birth (jus soli and jus sanguinis) and naturalization processes.
The UK also has comprehensive legislation governing citizenship acquisition, including by birth, descent, registration, and naturalization.

content.legal_text: 25. (1) The following persons are citizens of Nigeria by birth-namely-(a) every person born in Nigeria before the date of independence, either of whose parents or any of whose grandparents belongs or belonged to a community indigenous to Nigeria; Provided that a person shall not become a citizen of Nigeria by virtue of this section if neither of his parents nor any of his grandparents was born in Nigeria.(b) every person born in Nigeria after the date of independence either of whose parents or any of whose grandparents is a citizen of Nigeria; and(c) every person born outside Nigeria either of whose parents is a citizen of Nigeria.(2) In this section, "the date of independence" means the 1st day of October 1960.
content.plain_english: This section defines who is a Nigerian citizen by birth. It includes individuals born in Nigeria before October 1, 1960, if their parents or grandparents were from an indigenous Nigerian community (with a condition that at least one parent or grandparent must have been born in Nigeria). It also includes anyone born in Nigeria after October 1, 1960, if at least one parent or grandparent is a Nigerian citizen, and anyone born outside Nigeria if at least one parent is a Nigerian citizen. The 'date of independence' is explicitly stated as October 1, 1960.

case_laws.case_title: Mogaji v. The State
case_laws.relevance_summary: While not directly on citizenship, cases involving indigenous status or historical ties can sometimes touch upon the interpretation of 'community indigenous to Nigeria'.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though primarily about dual citizenship for elected officials, often involves the foundational understanding of who is a citizen by birth.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Constitution grants citizenship to all persons born or naturalized in the US and subject to its jurisdiction (jus soli), similar to Nigeria's post-independence provision for birth in Nigeria.

India's citizenship laws also define citizenship by birth (jus soli, with modifications) and by descent (jus sanguinis), similar to Nigeria's provisions for those born outside the country to Nigerian parents.

content.legal_text: The following persons are citizens of Nigeria by birth-namely-(a) every person born in Nigeria before the date of independence, either of whose parents or any of whose grandparents belongs or belonged to a community indigenous to Nigeria; Provided that a person shall not become a citizen of Nigeria by virtue of this section if neither of his parents nor any of his grandparents was born in Nigeria.(b) every person born in Nigeria after the date of independence either of whose parents or any of whose grandparents is a citizen of Nigeria; and(c) every person born outside Nigeria either of whose parents is a citizen of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: This part specifies three categories of people who are citizens by birth: (a) those born in Nigeria before October 1, 1960, whose parents or grandparents were from an indigenous Nigerian community, provided at least one parent or grandparent was also born in Nigeria; (b) those born in Nigeria after October 1, 1960, if at least one parent or grandparent is a Nigerian citizen; and (c) those born outside Nigeria if at least one parent is a Nigerian citizen.

case_laws.case_title: Mogaji v. The State
case_laws.relevance_summary: Relevant for interpreting 'community indigenous to Nigeria' and historical aspects of citizenship.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The principle of 'jus soli' (citizenship by birth in the territory) is a cornerstone of US citizenship, similar to Nigeria's post-independence provision.
The UK's Act defines citizenship by birth in the UK (with parental conditions) and by descent, reflecting both jus soli and jus sanguinis principles.

content.legal_text: every person born in Nigeria before the date of independence, either of whose parents or any of whose grandparents belongs or belonged to a community indigenous to Nigeria; Provided that a person shall not become a citizen of Nigeria by virtue of this section if neither of his parents nor any of his grandparents was born in Nigeria.
content.plain_english: A person born in Nigeria before October 1, 1960, is a citizen by birth if one of their parents or grandparents belonged to an indigenous Nigerian community. However, this rule doesn't apply if none of their parents or grandparents were born in Nigeria.

case_laws.case_title: Mogaji v. The State
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though not directly on citizenship, deals with the concept of indigenous communities and historical ties, which is relevant to interpreting this provision.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's initial citizenship laws also had provisions for persons born before its independence, often linking to parental or ancestral ties to the territory.
Historical British nationality law often linked citizenship to birth within the British Empire or descent from British subjects, which influenced post-colonial citizenship definitions.

content.legal_text: every person born in Nigeria after the date of independence either of whose parents or any of whose grandparents is a citizen of Nigeria; and
content.plain_english: Anyone born in Nigeria after October 1, 1960, is a citizen by birth if at least one of their parents or grandparents is a Nigerian citizen.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while focused on dual citizenship, implicitly relies on the definition of who is a citizen by birth, which is established here.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US grants citizenship to all persons born within its territory, regardless of parentage, a strong 'jus soli' principle.
Canada also largely follows 'jus soli', granting citizenship to most individuals born on Canadian soil.

content.legal_text: every person born outside Nigeria either of whose parents is a citizen of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: A person born outside Nigeria is a citizen by birth if at least one of their parents is a Nigerian citizen.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while focused on dual citizenship, implicitly relies on the definition of who is a citizen by birth, which is established here.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK grants citizenship by descent to children born outside the UK if one parent is a British citizen, similar to Nigeria's 'jus sanguinis' provision.
India's law also provides for citizenship by descent for those born outside India to Indian citizen parents.

content.legal_text: In this section, "the date of independence" means the 1st day of October 1960.
content.plain_english: For the purpose of this section, 'the date of independence' refers to October 1, 1960.

content.legal_text: 26. (1) Subject to the provisions of section 28 of this Constitution, a person to whom the provisions of this section apply may be registered as a citizen of Nigeria, if the President is satisfied that -(a) he is a person of good character;(b) he has shown a clear intention of his desire to be domiciled in Nigeria; and(c) he has taken the Oath of Allegiance prescribed in the Seventh Schedule to this Constitution.(2) the provisions of this section shall apply to-(a) any woman who is or has been married to a citizen of Nigeria; or(b) every person of full age and capacity born outside Nigeria any of whose grandparents is a citizen of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: This section outlines how certain individuals can become Nigerian citizens by registration, provided they meet specific conditions and are not disqualified by Section 28. The President must be satisfied that the applicant has good character, intends to live permanently in Nigeria, and has taken the Oath of Allegiance. This process is available to women married to Nigerian citizens (or formerly married) and to adults born outside Nigeria whose grandparents were Nigerian citizens.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: While primarily about deportation and fundamental rights, this case highlights the importance of due process in matters affecting citizenship status and the powers of the executive.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK allows for registration as a citizen for certain categories, including spouses of British citizens and individuals with a British parent or grandparent, subject to character and residency requirements.

India's Act provides for citizenship by registration for persons of Indian origin, spouses of Indian citizens, and minor children of Indian citizens, with similar conditions like oath and good character.

content.legal_text: Subject to the provisions of section 28 of this Constitution, a person to whom the provisions of this section apply may be registered as a citizen of Nigeria, if the President is satisfied that -(a) he is a person of good character;(b) he has shown a clear intention of his desire to be domiciled in Nigeria; and(c) he has taken the Oath of Allegiance prescribed in the Seventh Schedule to this Constitution.
content.plain_english: Under this section, and considering Section 28, the President can register someone as a Nigerian citizen if they meet certain criteria: they must have good character, clearly intend to live permanently in Nigeria, and have taken the Oath of Allegiance.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though not directly on registration, underscores the importance of due process and the executive's powers in citizenship matters.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US naturalization process requires applicants to demonstrate good moral character, an intention to reside permanently, and to take an Oath of Allegiance.
The UK also requires applicants for registration or naturalization to be of good character and to take an oath of allegiance.

content.legal_text: he is a person of good character;
content.plain_english: The applicant must be considered a person of good character.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases involving the executive's assessment of an individual's character or suitability for residence can be relevant here.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: US naturalization requires applicants to demonstrate 'good moral character'.
The UK also has a 'good character' requirement for most citizenship applications.

content.legal_text: he has shown a clear intention of his desire to be domiciled in Nigeria; and
content.plain_english: The applicant must demonstrate a clear intention to make Nigeria their permanent home.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, concerning a person's right to reside in Nigeria, touches on the concept of domicile and the government's power to determine residency.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK requires applicants for naturalization to demonstrate an 'intention to reside' in the UK.
Canadian citizenship law includes a 'residence requirement' which implies an intention to make Canada one's home.

content.legal_text: he has taken the Oath of Allegiance prescribed in the Seventh Schedule to this Constitution.
content.plain_english: The applicant must have taken the Oath of Allegiance as outlined in the Constitution's Seventh Schedule.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: All US naturalization applicants must take an Oath of Allegiance to the United States.
Applicants for British citizenship are required to take an oath of allegiance and a pledge.

content.legal_text: the provisions of this section shall apply to-(a) any woman who is or has been married to a citizen of Nigeria; or(b) every person of full age and capacity born outside Nigeria any of whose grandparents is a citizen of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: This section applies to (a) any woman who is currently or was previously married to a Nigerian citizen, and (b) any adult born outside Nigeria whose grandparents were Nigerian citizens.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while focused on dual citizenship, implicitly relies on the definition of who is a citizen by birth, which is established here.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US provides expedited naturalization paths for spouses of US citizens.
The UK allows for registration as a British citizen for spouses of British citizens and individuals with a British grandparent.

content.legal_text: any woman who is or has been married to a citizen of Nigeria; or
content.plain_english: This applies to any woman who is currently married to a Nigerian citizen or was previously married to one.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Spouses of US citizens generally have a shorter residency requirement for naturalization.
The Indian Act allows a person who is married to a citizen of India to apply for citizenship by registration.

content.legal_text: every person of full age and capacity born outside Nigeria any of whose grandparents is a citizen of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: This applies to any adult (of full legal age and mental capacity) who was born outside Nigeria and has at least one grandparent who is a Nigerian citizen.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK allows for registration as a British citizen for individuals with a British grandparent, subject to certain conditions.
Ireland offers citizenship by descent through grandparents for those born outside Ireland, often requiring registration in a foreign births register.

content.legal_text: 27. (1) Subject to the provisions of section 28 of this Constitution, any person who is qualified in accordance with the provisions of this section may apply to the President for the same of a certificate of naturalisation.(2) No person shall be qualified to apply for the grant of a certificate or naturalisation, unless he satisfies the President that -(a) he is a person of full age and capacity;(b) he is a person of good character;(c) he has shown a clear intention of his desire to be domiciled in Nigeria;(d) he is, in the opinion of the Governor of the State where he is or he proposes to be resident, acceptable to the local community in which he is to live permanently, and has been assimilated into the way of life of Nigerians in that part of the Federation;(e) he is a person who has made or is capable of making useful contribution to the advancement; progress and well-being of Nigeria;(f) he has taken the Oath of Allegiance prescribed in the Seventh Schedule to this Constitution; and(g) he has, immediately preceding the date of his application, either-(i) resided in Nigeria for a continuous period of fifteen years; or(ii) resided in Nigeria continuously for a period of twelve months, and during the period of twenty years immediately preceding that period of twelve months has resided in Nigeria for periods amounting in the aggregate to not less than fifteen years.
content.plain_english: This section outlines the process for obtaining Nigerian citizenship through naturalization, subject to Section 28. An applicant must apply to the President and satisfy several strict conditions: they must be an adult of sound mind, of good character, intend to live permanently in Nigeria, be accepted by their local community and assimilated into Nigerian life, be able to contribute to Nigeria's development, take the Oath of Allegiance, and meet specific long-term residency requirements (either 15 continuous years or 12 continuous months preceded by 15 years of aggregate residency over 20 years).

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though focused on deportation, highlights the executive's powers and the rights of individuals concerning residency and citizenship status, which are foundational to naturalization.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US naturalization process has similar requirements including age, good moral character, residency, knowledge of English and civics, and an oath of allegiance.
Germany's naturalization process also emphasizes integration, language proficiency, and a commitment to the democratic order, similar to Nigeria's assimilation and contribution requirements.

content.legal_text: Subject to the provisions of section 28 of this Constitution, any person who is qualified in accordance with the provisions of this section may apply to the President for the same of a certificate of naturalisation.
content.plain_english: Anyone who meets the qualifications in this section, and is not excluded by Section 28, can apply to the President for a certificate of naturalization.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Individuals apply to USCIS (under the Department of Homeland Security) for naturalization, which is then approved by the government.
Applications for naturalization are made to the Home Secretary, who has discretion in granting citizenship.

content.legal_text: No person shall be qualified to apply for the grant of a certificate or naturalisation, unless he satisfies the President that -(a) he is a person of full age and capacity;(b) he is a person of good character;(c) he has shown a clear intention of his desire to be domiciled in Nigeria;(d) he is, in the opinion of the Governor of the State where he is or he proposes to be resident, acceptable to the local community in which he is to live permanently, and has been assimilated into the way of life of Nigerians in that part of the Federation;(e) he is a person who has made or is capable of making useful contribution to the advancement; progress and well-being of Nigeria;(f) he has taken the Oath of Allegiance prescribed in the Seventh Schedule to this Constitution; and(g) he has, immediately preceding the date of his application, either-(i) resided in Nigeria for a continuous period of fifteen years; or(ii) resided in Nigeria continuously for a period of twelve months, and during the period of twenty years immediately preceding that period of twelve months has resided in Nigeria for periods amounting in the aggregate to not less than fifteen years.
content.plain_english: To qualify for naturalization, an applicant must convince the President that they meet all the following conditions: (a) they are an adult of sound mind; (b) they have good character; (c) they clearly intend to live permanently in Nigeria; (d) they are accepted by their local community and have adopted Nigerian ways of life, as confirmed by the State Governor; (e) they can contribute positively to Nigeria's development; (f) they have taken the Oath of Allegiance; and (g) they have lived in Nigeria for either 15 continuous years immediately before applying, or for 12 continuous months immediately before applying, preceded by a total of at least 15 years of residency within the last 20 years.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while not directly on naturalization, highlights the executive's discretion and the importance of meeting residency and character requirements for citizenship-related matters.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: US naturalization requires continuous residence, good moral character, English language proficiency, knowledge of US government and history, and an oath of allegiance.
Canada's naturalization requirements include residency, language ability, knowledge of Canada, and an oath of citizenship.

content.legal_text: he is a person of full age and capacity;
content.plain_english: The applicant must be an adult and legally capable (of sound mind).

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Applicants for naturalization must generally be 18 years or older.
Applicants for naturalization must generally be 18 years or older.

content.legal_text: he is a person of good character;
content.plain_english: The applicant must be considered a person of good character.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases involving the executive's assessment of an individual's character or suitability for residence can be relevant here.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: US naturalization requires applicants to demonstrate 'good moral character'.
The UK also has a 'good character' requirement for most citizenship applications.

content.legal_text: he has shown a clear intention of his desire to be domiciled in Nigeria;
content.plain_english: The applicant must clearly show they intend to live permanently in Nigeria.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, concerning a person's right to reside in Nigeria, touches on the concept of domicile and the government's power to determine residency.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK requires applicants for naturalization to declare an 'intention to reside' in the UK.
Canadian citizenship law includes a 'residence requirement' which implies an intention to make Canada one's home.

content.legal_text: he is, in the opinion of the Governor of the State where he is or he proposes to be resident, acceptable to the local community in which he is to live permanently, and has been assimilated into the way of life of Nigerians in that part of the Federation;
content.plain_english: The Governor of the state where the applicant lives or plans to live must confirm that the applicant is accepted by the local community and has integrated into the Nigerian way of life in that area.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though not directly on naturalization, highlights the executive's discretion and the importance of meeting residency and character requirements for citizenship-related matters, including community acceptance.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: German naturalization law places a strong emphasis on integration into German society and commitment to its democratic values.
French naturalization requires proof of 'assimilation' into the French community, including knowledge of French language and culture.

content.legal_text: he is a person who has made or is capable of making useful contribution to the advancement; progress and well-being of Nigeria;
content.plain_english: The applicant must have already contributed or be capable of contributing positively to Nigeria's development, progress, and welfare.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: While not a general requirement, some countries like Canada have provisions for granting citizenship in 'exceptional circumstances' to individuals who have made outstanding contributions.
Australia has provisions for 'special eligibility' for citizenship for individuals of special talent or global distinction, implying a contribution to the nation.

content.legal_text: he has taken the Oath of Allegiance prescribed in the Seventh Schedule to this Constitution; and
content.plain_english: The applicant must have taken the Oath of Allegiance as specified in the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: All US naturalization applicants must take an Oath of Allegiance to the United States.
Applicants for British citizenship are required to take an oath of allegiance and a pledge.

content.legal_text: he has, immediately preceding the date of his application, either-(i) resided in Nigeria for a continuous period of fifteen years; or(ii) resided in Nigeria continuously for a period of twelve months, and during the period of twenty years immediately preceding that period of twelve months has resided in Nigeria for periods amounting in the aggregate to not less than fifteen years.
content.plain_english: Immediately before applying, the applicant must have either lived in Nigeria continuously for 15 years, or lived in Nigeria continuously for 12 months, and also lived in Nigeria for a total of at least 15 years within the 20 years preceding that 12-month period.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US generally requires 5 years of continuous residence (3 years for spouses of US citizens) before applying for naturalization.
The UK generally requires 5 years of continuous residence (3 years for spouses of British citizens) before applying for naturalization.

content.legal_text: resided in Nigeria for a continuous period of fifteen years; or
content.plain_english: The applicant must have lived in Nigeria continuously for fifteen years immediately before their application.

content.legal_text: resided in Nigeria continuously for a period of twelve months, and during the period of twenty years immediately preceding that period of twelve months has resided in Nigeria for periods amounting in the aggregate to not less than fifteen years.
content.plain_english: Alternatively, the applicant must have lived in Nigeria continuously for 12 months immediately before applying, and also have accumulated at least 15 years of residency in Nigeria over the 20 years prior to that 12-month period.

content.legal_text: 28. (1) Subject to the other provisions of this section, a person shall forfeit forthwith his Nigerian citizenship if, not being a citizen of Nigeria by birth, he acquires or retains the citizenship or nationality of a country, other than Nigeria, of which he is not a citizen by birth.(2) Any registration of a person as a citizen of Nigeria or the grant of a certificate of naturalisation to a person who is a citizen of a country other than Nigeria at the time of such registration or grant shall, if he is not a citizen by birth of that other country, be conditional upon effective renunciation of the citizenship or nationality of that other country within a period of not more than five months from the date of such registration or grant.
content.plain_english: This section generally prohibits dual citizenship for those who are not Nigerian citizens by birth. (1) If someone who isn't a Nigerian citizen by birth acquires or keeps citizenship of another country (unless they are a citizen of that other country by birth), they immediately lose their Nigerian citizenship. (2) If a non-birth Nigerian citizen is registered or naturalized while also being a citizen of another country (and not a birth citizen of that other country), their Nigerian citizenship is conditional on them formally renouncing their other citizenship within five months.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This landmark case extensively discussed the implications of dual citizenship for elected officials in Nigeria, directly interpreting Section 28.

case_laws.case_title: Okoro v. The State
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases dealing with the loss or acquisition of citizenship often refer to the principles laid out in Section 28.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: India generally does not allow dual citizenship, requiring individuals to renounce foreign citizenship upon acquiring Indian citizenship.
Germany generally requires renunciation of prior citizenship upon naturalization, though exceptions exist, particularly for EU citizens.

content.legal_text: Subject to the other provisions of this section, a person shall forfeit forthwith his Nigerian citizenship if, not being a citizen of Nigeria by birth, he acquires or retains the citizenship or nationality of a country, other than Nigeria, of which he is not a citizen by birth.
content.plain_english: Unless other parts of this section state otherwise, a person who is not a Nigerian citizen by birth will immediately lose their Nigerian citizenship if they gain or keep citizenship of another country, unless they are a citizen of that other country by birth.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case directly addresses the forfeiture of Nigerian citizenship due to the acquisition or retention of foreign citizenship by non-birth citizens.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Indian law mandates that if a person voluntarily acquires citizenship of another country, their Indian citizenship is automatically terminated.
Japan generally does not permit dual nationality and requires individuals to choose one citizenship by a certain age.

content.legal_text: Any registration of a person as a citizen of Nigeria or the grant of a certificate of naturalisation to a person who is a citizen of a country other than Nigeria at the time of such registration or grant shall, if he is not a citizen by birth of that other country, be conditional upon effective renunciation of the citizenship or nationality of that other country within a period of not more than five months from the date of such registration or grant.
content.plain_english: If someone who is already a citizen of another country (and not a birth citizen of that country) is registered or naturalized as a Nigerian citizen, their Nigerian citizenship is temporary. They must formally give up their other citizenship within five months of becoming a Nigerian citizen, otherwise, their Nigerian citizenship will be invalid.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case directly addresses the implications of dual citizenship for elected officials and the requirement for renunciation.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Canada generally requires naturalization applicants to renounce their prior citizenship, though the process and timing may differ.
Australia may require renunciation of foreign citizenship for naturalization, depending on the country of origin and specific circumstances.

content.legal_text: 29. (1) Any citizen of Nigeria of full age who wishes to renounce his Nigerian citizenship shall make a declaration in the prescribed manner for the renunciation.(2) The President shall cause the declaration made under subsection (1) of this section to be registered and upon such registration, the person who made the declaration shall cease to be a citizen of Nigeria.(3) The President may withhold the registration of any declaration made under subsection (1) of this section if-(a) the declaration is made during any war in which Nigeria is physically involved; or(b) in his opinion, it is otherwise contrary to public policy.(4) For the purposes of subsection (1) of this section.(a) "full age" means the age of eighteen years and above;(b) any woman who is married shall be deemed to be of full age.
content.plain_english: This section allows Nigerian citizens of full age to renounce their citizenship. (1) An adult Nigerian citizen can declare their intention to renounce citizenship in the specified way. (2) Once the President registers this declaration, the person who made the declaration stops being a Nigerian citizen. (3) However, the President can refuse to register the renunciation if Nigeria is involved in a war or if it's against public policy. (4) For this section, 'full age' means 18 years or older, and any married woman is considered to be of full age.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: US citizens have a right to renounce their citizenship, typically through a formal declaration at a US embassy or consulate abroad.
British citizens can renounce their citizenship by making a declaration, which is then registered by the Home Secretary.

content.legal_text: Any citizen of Nigeria of full age who wishes to renounce his Nigerian citizenship shall make a declaration in the prescribed manner for the renunciation.
content.plain_english: Any adult Nigerian citizen who wants to give up their citizenship must formally declare their intention in the way specified by law.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: US citizens must make a formal declaration of renunciation before a consular officer.
Canadian citizens can renounce their citizenship by applying to the Minister, provided certain conditions are met.

content.legal_text: The President shall cause the declaration made under subsection (1) of this section to be registered and upon such registration, the person who made the declaration shall cease to be a citizen of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: The President is responsible for registering the renunciation declaration. Once registered, the person who made the declaration is no longer a Nigerian citizen.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Upon approval of renunciation, a Certificate of Loss of Nationality is issued, formally ending US citizenship.
Renunciation takes effect upon registration of the declaration by the Home Secretary.

content.legal_text: The President may withhold the registration of any declaration made under subsection (1) of this section if-(a) the declaration is made during any war in which Nigeria is physically involved; or(b) in his opinion, it is otherwise contrary to public policy.
content.plain_english: The President has the power to refuse to register a renunciation if Nigeria is currently involved in a war, or if the President believes it would be against the public interest.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: While the right to renounce is fundamental, it can be restricted during wartime or if it would be contrary to national security.
The Home Secretary may refuse to register a renunciation if it is not in the public interest.

content.legal_text: the declaration is made during any war in which Nigeria is physically involved; or
content.plain_english: The President can refuse registration if the renunciation is declared while Nigeria is actively engaged in a war.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Renunciation of US citizenship during wartime can be viewed as an act of disloyalty and may be subject to restrictions.

content.legal_text: in his opinion, it is otherwise contrary to public policy.
content.plain_english: The President can refuse registration if, in their judgment, it goes against the public interest.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The Home Secretary can refuse to register a renunciation if it is not in the public interest.

content.legal_text: For the purposes of subsection (1) of this section.(a) "full age" means the age of eighteen years and above;(b) any woman who is married shall be deemed to be of full age.
content.plain_english: For the purpose of renouncing citizenship under subsection (1), 'full age' means 18 years or older. Additionally, any married woman is considered to be of full age, regardless of her actual age.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: State laws on age of majority
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The age of majority in the US is generally 18, but the provision deeming married women of full age is less common in modern US law.
The age of majority in the UK is 18. The specific provision for married women being deemed of full age is a historical artifact in many legal systems, less common in contemporary law.

content.legal_text: "full age" means the age of eighteen years and above;
content.plain_english: For this section, 'full age' is defined as 18 years old or older.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: State laws on age of majority
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The age of majority in most US states is 18 years.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Provincial/Territorial laws on age of majority
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The age of majority in Canada is typically 18 or 19, depending on the province/territory.

content.legal_text: any woman who is married shall be deemed to be of full age.
content.plain_english: Any woman who is married is legally considered to be of full age, regardless of her actual chronological age.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Historically, some legal systems, including in India, had provisions where marriage conferred adult status, though modern laws tend to standardize the age of majority.
Under historical common law, marriage often conferred legal adulthood on women, even if they were chronologically minors, though this has largely been superseded by modern legislation.

content.legal_text: 30. (1) The President may deprive a person, other than a person who is a citizen of Nigeria by birth or by registration, of his citizenship, if he is satisfied that such a person has, within a period of seven years after becoming naturalised, been sentenced to imprisonment for a term of not less than three years.(2) The President shall deprive a person, other than a person who is citizen of Nigeria by birth, of his citizenship, if he is satisfied from the records of proceedings of a court of law or other tribunal or after due inquiry in accordance with regulations made by him, that -(a) the person has shown himself by act or speech to be disloyal towards the Federal Republic of Nigeria; or(b) the person has, during any war in which Nigeria was engaged, unlawfully traded with the enemy or been engaged in or associated with any business that was in the opinion of the president carried on in such a manner as to assist the enemy of Nigeria in that war, or unlawfully communicated with such enemy to the detriment of or with intent to cause damage to the interest of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: This section outlines when the President can revoke a person's Nigerian citizenship. (1) The President can revoke the citizenship of a naturalized person (but not a citizen by birth or registration) if, within seven years of naturalization, they are sentenced to at least three years in prison. (2) The President *must* revoke the citizenship of any non-birth citizen if it's proven (through court records or an inquiry) that they have shown disloyalty to Nigeria through actions or words, or if during a war, they unlawfully traded with or assisted an enemy, or communicated with an enemy to Nigeria's detriment.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though pre-dating the specific wording, deals with the executive's power to affect a person's citizenship status and the importance of due process.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while focused on dual citizenship, touches on the fundamental aspects of citizenship and its potential loss.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK allows for deprivation of citizenship (especially for naturalized citizens) on grounds of serious crime, fraud, or actions seriously prejudicial to the UK's interests.
The US provides for denaturalization (revocation of naturalized citizenship) for fraud, misrepresentation, or certain acts of disloyalty.

content.legal_text: The President may deprive a person, other than a person who is a citizen of Nigeria by birth or by registration, of his citizenship, if he is satisfied that such a person has, within a period of seven years after becoming naturalised, been sentenced to imprisonment for a term of not less than three years.
content.plain_english: The President has the discretion to take away the citizenship of a naturalized person (but not someone who is a citizen by birth or registration) if, within seven years of becoming naturalized, that person was sentenced to at least three years in prison.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though not directly on this specific clause, highlights the executive's power to affect citizenship status and the need for due process.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK allows for deprivation of citizenship if a person has been convicted of a serious criminal offence.
Canada allows for revocation of citizenship for serious criminal offenses, particularly those related to national security or organized crime.

content.legal_text: The President shall deprive a person, other than a person who is citizen of Nigeria by birth, of his citizenship, if he is satisfied from the records of proceedings of a court of law or other tribunal or after due inquiry in accordance with regulations made by him, that -(a) the person has shown himself by act or speech to be disloyal towards the Federal Republic of Nigeria; or(b) the person has, during any war in which Nigeria was engaged, unlawfully traded with the enemy or been engaged in or associated with any business that was in the opinion of the president carried on in such a manner as to assist the enemy of Nigeria in that war, or unlawfully communicated with such enemy to the detriment of or with intent to cause damage to the interest of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: The President *must* take away the citizenship of any person who is not a citizen by birth if it is proven (through court records, tribunal proceedings, or an official inquiry) that: (a) the person has demonstrated disloyalty to Nigeria through their actions or words; or (b) during a war involving Nigeria, the person illegally traded with or assisted the enemy, or was involved in a business that the President believes helped the enemy, or illegally communicated with the enemy in a way that harmed or intended to harm Nigeria's interests.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though pre-dating the specific wording, deals with the executive's power to affect a person's citizenship status and the importance of due process in such serious matters.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK allows for deprivation of citizenship if it is conducive to the public good, particularly on grounds of national security or serious harm to UK interests.
The US provides for denaturalization for acts of disloyalty, treason, or participation in certain hostile activities against the US.

content.legal_text: the person has shown himself by act or speech to be disloyal towards the Federal Republic of Nigeria; or
content.plain_english: The person has demonstrated disloyalty to Nigeria through their actions or words.

case_laws.case_title: Shugaba Darman v. Minister of Internal Affairs
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, though not directly on this clause, highlights the executive's power to affect citizenship status and the need for due process in such serious matters.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Deprivation of citizenship can occur if a person has acted in a manner seriously prejudicial to the vital interests of the UK.
Denaturalization can occur for acts of disloyalty, such as committing treason or attempting to overthrow the government.

content.legal_text: the person has, during any war in which Nigeria was engaged, unlawfully traded with the enemy or been engaged in or associated with any business that was in the opinion of the president carried on in such a manner as to assist the enemy of Nigeria in that war, or unlawfully communicated with such enemy to the detriment of or with intent to cause damage to the interest of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: During a war involving Nigeria, the person illegally traded with or assisted the enemy, or was involved in a business that the President believes helped the enemy, or illegally communicated with the enemy in a way that harmed or intended to harm Nigeria's interests.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Immigration and Nationality Act, Espionage Act
international_comparisons.similarity_note: US law provides for denaturalization and severe penalties for acts of treason, espionage, or aiding an enemy during wartime.
Deprivation of citizenship can occur for acts seriously prejudicial to the UK's interests, which would include wartime collaboration or aiding an enemy.

hierarchy.section_title: Persons deemed to be citizens of Nigeria

content.legal_text: 31. For the purposes of this Chapter, a parent or grandparent of a person shall be deemed to be a citizen of Nigeria if at the time of the birth of that person such parent or grandparent would have possessed that status by birth if he had been alive on the date of independence; and in this section, "the date of independence" has the meaning assigned to it in section 25 (2) of this Constitution.
content.plain_english: For the rules in this chapter, a person's parent or grandparent is considered a Nigerian citizen if, at the time of the person's birth, that parent or grandparent would have been a citizen by birth had they been alive on October 1, 1960 (Nigeria's independence day). The 'date of independence' here means the same as defined in Section 25(2).

case_laws.case_title: Mogaji v. The State
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases dealing with indigenous status and historical ties are relevant to understanding how pre-independence lineage is interpreted for citizenship purposes.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Abubakar
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case, while focused on dual citizenship, implicitly relies on the foundational understanding of who is a citizen by birth, which is established here.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: India's citizenship laws also contain provisions for 'deemed citizens' or those whose ancestors would have been citizens at the time of independence, to establish lineage.
Ireland has provisions for citizenship by descent based on parents or grandparents who were Irish citizens at the time of the applicant's birth, or who would have been if alive.

content.legal_text: 32. (1) The president may make regulations, not inconsistent with this Chapter, prescribing all matters which are required or permitted to be prescribed or which are necessary or convenient to be prescribed for carrying out or giving effect to the provisions of this Chapter, and for granting special immigrant status with full residential rights to non-Nigerian spouses of citizens of Nigeria who do not wish to acquire Nigerian citizenship.(2) Any regulations made by the president pursuant to the provisions of this section shall be laid before the National Assembly.
content.plain_english: This section grants the President the authority to create regulations to implement this chapter on citizenship. (1) The President can make rules that are consistent with this chapter to cover any necessary or convenient matters for putting the chapter into effect. This also includes granting special immigrant status with full residency rights to non-Nigerian spouses of Nigerian citizens who choose not to become Nigerian citizens themselves. (2) Any such regulations made by the President must be presented to the National Assembly for review.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US President and relevant agencies (like DHS) have broad powers to issue regulations and executive orders to implement immigration and nationality laws.
The Home Secretary has powers to make immigration rules and regulations to give effect to nationality laws, which are subject to parliamentary scrutiny.

content.legal_text: The president may make regulations, not inconsistent with this Chapter, prescribing all matters which are required or permitted to be prescribed or which are necessary or convenient to be prescribed for carrying out or giving effect to the provisions of this Chapter, and for granting special immigrant status with full residential rights to non-Nigerian spouses of citizens of Nigeria who do not wish to acquire Nigerian citizenship.
content.plain_english: The President has the authority to create regulations that align with this chapter. These regulations can cover anything required or allowed by the chapter, or anything necessary to implement it. This includes the power to grant special immigrant status and full residency rights to foreign spouses of Nigerian citizens who prefer not to become Nigerian citizens themselves.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The US Department of Homeland Security issues regulations to implement immigration laws, including provisions for spouses of US citizens.
Canada's immigration laws allow for regulations to be made concerning family reunification and residency rights for spouses.

content.legal_text: Any regulations made by the president pursuant to the provisions of this section shall be laid before the National Assembly.
content.plain_english: All regulations created by the President under this section must be presented to the National Assembly for their review.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: In the UK, statutory instruments (delegated legislation) made by ministers are typically 'laid before Parliament' for scrutiny, often subject to annulment or affirmative resolution procedures.
In India, rules and regulations made by the executive are often required to be laid before Parliament, allowing for legislative oversight.
